evidence.title: Vancomycin Powder Combined With Autogenous Bone Graft as a Prevention for Post-operative Infection for Spine Surgery
evidence.snippet: Title: Vancomycin Powder Combined With Autogenous Bone Graft as a Prevention for Post-operative Infection for Spine Surgery

evidence.title: Brief Summary
evidence.snippet: Deep surgical site infection (DSSI) is one of the most challenging complications for spinal surgeons and can lead to a poor clinical outcome. This ambispective study was designed to examine the effect of vancomycin powder mixed with autogenous bone graft and bone substitute on preventing deep surgical site infection (DSSI) in degenerative lumbar fusion surgeries as well as any interference with bony fusion.

evidence.title: Study Status
evidence.snippet: Status: RECRUITING

evidence.title: Conditions & Keywords
evidence.snippet: Conditions: Spinal Fusion, Spinal Stenosis, Spondylolisthesis | Keywords: Deep surgical site infection, Vancomycin-impregnated bone graft, Spine surgery, Degenerative lumbar disorder

evidence.title: Intervention: with U-VANCO (vancomycin hydrochloride)
evidence.snippet: DRUG: with U-VANCO (vancomycin hydrochloride) - 1 gm or 2 gm mixed with autogenous cancellous bone graft

evidence.title: Intervention: without U-VANCO (vancomycin hydrochloride)
evidence.snippet: DRUG: without U-VANCO (vancomycin hydrochloride) - no vancomycin added

evidence.title: Phase
evidence.snippet: Phase: NA

evidence.title: Vancomycin powder mixed with autogenous bone graft and bone substitute may decrease the deep surgical site infections in elective lumbar instrumented fusion surgery for degenerative disorders: a prospective randomized study.
evidence.snippet:
Title: Vancomycin powder mixed with autogenous bone graft and bone substitute may decrease the deep surgical site infections in elective lumbar instrumented fusion surgery for degenerative disorders: a prospective randomized study.
Authors: Wang Shih-Tien, Lin Hsi-Hsien, Yao Yu-Cheng et al.
Journal: The spine journal : official journal of the North American Spine Society (2025)
Abstract: BACKGROUND CONTEXT: Deep surgical site infections (DSSI) following lumbar instrumented fusion surgery are associated with considerable morbidity. Intraoperative application of vancomycin powder (VP) has been widely used to prevent DSSI; however, the effects of VP mixed with local autogenous bone graft (ABG) and bone substitute on DSSI prevention and bone fusion remains unclear. PURPOSE: To examine the effects of VP mixed with ABG and bone substitute on DSSI and fusion rate. STUDY DESIGN/SETTING:

opinions.reasoning: Vancomycin is a well-established antibiotic and not a peptide.

The evidence from the ChEMBL database clearly states that vancomycin hydrochloride is a small molecule drug, not a peptide therapeutic. This classification aligns with the critical rules provided, which stipulate that if a product contains 'peptide' in its name but is instead described as a nutritional formula, dietary supplement, or food, it should be classified as False for being a peptide therapeutic. In this case, vancomycin hydrochloride does not fit these criteria and is correctly identifi

According to the critical rules, if the active drug is not a peptide, then the primary intervention is not a peptide therapeutic. In this case, vancomycin hydrochloride is a small molecule, so the primary intervention is not a peptide therapeutic.
While vancomycin is an AMP because it directly kills bacteria, the trial's objective is wound healing (preventing infection) and bone fusion, not treating a specific infection.

Vancomycin hydrochloride, used in this trial, acts as a peptidoglycan inhibitor and has been shown to have direct killing effects against various bacteria such as Bacillus subtilis, Staphylococcus aureus, Enterococcus faecalis, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Escherichia coli. The mechanism of action involves the disruption of bacterial cell walls, leading to bacterial lysis or death. Therefore, based on its antimicrobial activity against pathogens, vancomycin hydrochloride qualifies as an antimicro

The trial involves the use of vancomycin hydrochloride, a known antimicrobial peptide that directly kills pathogens through its own biochemical action (membrane disruption). Furthermore, the trial is focused on preventing post-operative infection, which aligns with the targeting of infection in STEP 3.

fields.reconciler_reasoning: The trial's primary objective is to prevent post-operative infections in spine surgery. Vancomycin hydrochloride, used as an antimicrobial agent (AMP) with a mechanism of action that directly inhibits peptidoglycan synthesis and kills bacteria, aligns well with the goal of preventing surgical site infections. Although the broader purpose includes wound healing and bone fusion, the specific use of vancomycin is clearly aimed at infection prevention, supporting the classification as AMP(infection)

The evidence provided does not specify the exact mode of delivery for vancomycin powder. It is clear that it is administered intraoperatively, but without further details on whether it's injected into muscle tissue, subcutaneously, or infused intravenously, we cannot definitively assign a more specific category like IM, SC, or IV. Therefore, based on the information given and following the guidelines provided, Injection/Infusion - Other/Unspecified is the most appropriate choice.
The drug is administered topically as it is mixed with autogenous cancellous bone graft, which suggests a local application rather than systemic administration.

Given that the drug is combined with bone graft for local application, it could be applied in a manner that is not clearly defined as topical or systemic (e.g., injected into tissue). identificationModule.briefTitle: Vancomycin Powder Combined With Autogenous Bone Graft as a Prevention for Post-operative Infection for Spine Surgery
identificationModule.officialTitle: Vancomycin Powder Combined With Autogenous Bone Graft as a Prevention for Post-operative Infection for Spine Surgery

descriptionModule.briefSummary: Deep surgical site infection (DSSI) is one of the most challenging complications for spinal surgeons and can lead to a poor clinical outcome. This ambispective study was designed to examine the effect of vancomycin powder mixed with autogenous bone graft and bone substitute on preventing deep surgical site infection (DSSI) in degenerative lumbar fusion surgeries as well as any interference with bony fusion.

interventions.name: with U-VANCO (vancomycin hydrochloride)
interventions.description: 1 gm or 2 gm mixed with autogenous cancellous bone graft

secondaryOutcomes.description: Vancomycin concentration was checked for both the serum and drainage.
Vancomycin concentration was checked for both the serum and drainage.

eligibilityModule.eligibilityCriteria:
Inclusion Criteria:

* Patients who have instrumented spine fusion surgery due to failed conservative treatment (bed rest, medication, rehabilitation programs) for 3 to 6 months
* Patients who are willing to sign the surgical permit after the surgeon explains all surgical procedures along with the surgical risks
* Patients who fully understand the surgical procedures as well as the surgical risks and are willing to sign the surgical permit and the inform consent
* Patients who agree one more blood withdrawal after operation

Exclusion Criteria:

* Patients who agree to accept the spine surgery but do not agree to sign the inform consent
* Patients who have allergic reaction to vancomycin

references.citation: Wang ST, Lin HH, Yao YC, Huang N, Hsiung W, Chang MC, Liu CL, Chou PH. Vancomycin powder mixed with autogenous bone graft and bone substitute may decrease the deep surgical site infections in elective lumbar instrumented fusion surgery for degenerative disorders: a prospective randomized study. Spine J. 2025 Sep;25(9):1866-1876. doi: 10.1016/j.spinee.2025.05.001. Epub 2025 May 5.
